items.explanation: The subject is “bundle” — singular. The plural “affidavits” in the intervening phrase is the trap.
With neither/nor, the verb agrees with the nearer subject — “the county has.”
Possessive = its, no apostrophe. “It’s” only ever means “it is.” (“Its’” is not a word.)
The clause identifies WHICH claim — restrictive — so it takes “that” with no comma. “Which” needs a comma and a nonrestrictive role.
Singular noun ending in s takes ’s — witness’s. The bare apostrophe belongs to plurals.
To influence = affect. (Verb-“effect” means to bring about — “to effect a change” — which is not the sense here.)
American style: the period goes INSIDE the closing quotation mark, no exceptions.
Park “only” next to what it limits: the settlement releases ONLY those claims. Before the verb, it (literally) says releasing is the only thing the settlement does.
-ly adverbs never take a hyphen — the -ly already does the connecting.
Before the noun “facility,” the compound modifier hyphenates: cash-advance facility. (The middle option is open; the last uses an en dash — wrong mark.)
Statutes speak in the present: “the statute provides.” Past tense implies the provision is gone.
Two independent clauses need more than a comma — the semicolon fixes the splice. (A spaced dash isn’t house style; em dashes are unspaced.)
Institutions are singular in American legal prose: the agency HAS. (Note the sentence already says “its” — the verb must match.)
The pronoun is the SUBJECT of “reported” — so “who.” Case is set by the pronoun’s role in its own clause.
A rule or tenet is a PRINCIPLE. “Principal” is the adjective (main) or a person/sum.
The appositive “Marcus Webb” already has its closing comma — it needs its OPENING one too. Paired punctuation fails in halves.
An interruption opened with an em dash must close with one. A comma can’t close a dash.
“Each” is singular, even trailed by “of the claims.”
No object → lie. “The burden lies with the movant.” Lay needs something being laid.
“Both” means two defendants — plural possessive: defendants’. The apostrophe’s position states how many defendants there are.
A court capitalizes itself: “this Court reviews.” Lowercase is for other, generic courts (“the district court”).
Training, reporting, … monitoring — the third item must stay a gerund to match the series.
A full date used as a modifier keeps the comma after the year: “an August 9, 2023, order.” Dropping it is the natural typo — Judge Grant never does.
Of a tort = TORTIOUS. Tortuous means winding; torturous means agonizing. Spell-check accepts all three.
The real subject follows the verb: two grounds ARE. “There is” + plural is the spoken-English habit to un-learn in writing.
A university is an institutional singular — “it would appeal.”
A colon can’t interrupt a clause mid-verb. Either drop it (“include duty, breach…”) or complete the clause first (“include three things: …”).
Sharing the base word “based,” the first modifier keeps a hanging hyphen: “content- and viewpoint-based.”
Makes certain = ensures. Insure is what carriers do to property; assure is what you do to people.
The interrupting attribution — “the county says” — needs its CLOSING comma: “The permit, the county says, was issued in error.” Grant’s signature construction.
“Along with two aides” doesn’t pluralize the subject — the senator WAS subpoenaed. Only “and” builds a plural subject.
Items with internal commas take semicolon separators: “Dr. Ellis, an economist; and Ms. Ford, a nurse.” Otherwise the economist reads as a third witness.

choices: Simmons cannot revive it on appeal.
items.explanation: The appeal didn’t waive anything — a person did. The clause after the participial opener must supply the actor who waived.
“Is comprised of” is the stock misuse. Either “is composed of twelve volumes” or “comprises twelve volumes.”
As a named sovereign party — the State of Georgia — “State” capitalizes. Lowercase is for adjectival uses like “state law.”
“Not only … but also” must bracket parallel units: enjoins not only ENFORCEMENT but also DISCOVERY. With the verb inside “not only,” the second half has nothing to mirror.
Introducing a list is the colon’s job. Semicolons separate — they never announce.
Serial comma before the final “and” — uniform house style in every opinion the corpus covers.
“It” floats between agency and contractor — in litigation, who destroyed records is the whole ballgame. Repeat the noun. (“The latter” is a lawyerism that makes readers count backward.)
Small counts are spelled out in judicial prose — “three days.” Numerals are for money, dates, statutes, and dense data.
The court drew a conclusion FROM evidence — that is inferring. Texts and speakers imply; readers and courts infer.
Measure possessive: six months’ imprisonment — plural measure, apostrophe after the s. (Grant: “twenty years’ imprisonment.”)
A full introductory clause takes a comma before the main clause — without it, “issued the parties” garden-paths the reader.
Mixed subjects: the verb follows the NEARER one — “the auditor was.” (Flip the order and “were” becomes right; proximity decides.)
Semicolons sit OUTSIDE closing quotation marks — the mirror image of the periods-and-commas-inside rule.
Compound modifier before a noun hyphenates: a first-impression question (a question of first impression stays open — position decides). The third option is an en dash — wrong mark.
What a court DID in a past case takes the past: “In Meyer, this Court held.” (The rule Meyer announced can live in the present: “Meyer requires notice.”)
“It is undisputed” → It’s. The apostrophe marks the contraction.
The fund DID lose value — nearly half of it. “Nearly lost” says the loss almost happened at all. One word’s position changes the fact asserted.
To go forward = proceed. To precede is to come before something. Autocorrect swaps these silently.
“The only argument that survives” — restrictive, so “that.” After “the only,” the clause is always doing identifying work.
American convention: the panel IS divided. To emphasize individuals, recast — “the panel’s members are divided.”
Close the dash pair: “The rule—though rarely invoked—remains binding.” Mixed dash-comma pairs are among the most common slips in edited drafts.
Commas go inside the closing quote in American style — “ajar,” not “ajar”,.
Objections are countable — fewer. Less is for mass quantities (less delay, less friction).
